markdown.markdown:
## Highlights - The definition of the supremum of a set and the Least Upper Bound axiom of $\R$ - The Archimedean Properties of $\R$ ## Preparation Leading up to the third and final axiom of $\R$, we make a few definitions. :::{.definition #def-lub} Let $S\subseteq\R$ be a subset of the real numbers. 1. We say $b\in\R$ is an *upper bound* of $S$ if for all $x\in S$, $b\geq x.$ If $S$ has an upper bound, we say $S$ is *bounded above*. 2. We say that $u\in\R$ the *least upper bound* or *supremum* of $S$ if for all upper bounds $b$ of $S$, $u\leq b$, and in this case we write $$u=\sup S.$$ 3. If $u$ is the least upper bound $S$ and $u\in S$, we say that $u$ is the *greatest element* of $S.$ ::: :::{.exercise #exr-lub-examples} 1. Write down three upper bounds for the interval $S=[0,3)$. Does $S$ have a least upper bound; if so what is it? Does $S$ have a greatest element; if so, what is it? 2. For each set below, say what it's supremum and greatest element are or say why it doesn't have one. a. $\left\{\frac{n}{n+1}\st n\in\N\right\}$ b. $(2,\infty)$ c. $\left\{\frac{1}{n}\st n\in\N\right\}$ d. $(-\infty,3]$ e. $\{x\st x^2\leq 3\}$ 3. Describe in your own words which subsets of $\R$ have upper bounds, which have least upper bounds, and which have greatest elements. This doesn't have to be precise; just give me the vibe. 4. **(Exploratory)** How would you change your answers to the previous problems if our universe were $\Q$ instead of $\R?$ ::: :::{.content-visible when-format="html"} The example of $S=[0,3)$ is illustrated below. You can play with the slider to help you think about what value(s) of $b$ might satisfy the definitions of upper bound, supremum, or greatest element.
::: :::{.exercise #exr-bounded-above-logical-symbols} Write "$S$ is bounded above" in logical symbolic notation; see if you can do so without using the term "upper bound". ::: :::{.exercise #exr-negate-lub} Suppose $b$ is an upper bound on a set $S$. What does it mean for $b$ to *not* be the *least* upper bound of $S$? ::: ## Least Upper Bounds Part 2e of @exr-lub-examples is meant to draw a contrast between how least upper bounds work in $\R$ versus how they work in $\Q.$ In particular, the problem asks, if $$S=\{x\st x^2\leq 3\}?$$ does $S$ have a least upper bound? Whether our universe is $\Q$ or $\R$ or some other ordered field, $S$ certainly has *an* upper bound; $b=2$, for example, is greater than any element of $S$ (that is, $2$ is greater than any number whose square is less than 3). But as you may have guessed, an ordered field has a number whose square is $3$ if and only if the set $S$ defined above has a *least* upper bound (in which case $\sup S$ is such a number). We have a sense that the since $3$ has no square root in $\Q$, there is something missing---a "hole" in the rationals that we plug by instead thinking about $\R$. And as this example shows, plugging such a hole can be related to the existence of a least upper bound on a set. This connection goes in the opposite direction as well. If we *assume* the supremum of $S$ exists, then we at least have a candidate for a number whose square is $3$ (actually proving this is more complicated than you might expect---see @thm-sqrts-exist below). This is all motivation for the final axiom we'll assume about the real nubers: a guarantee of the existence of least upper bounds that will have the effect of ensuring that any $\R$ will not have any "holes" in this sense, the way that $\Q$ does. :::{.theorem #thm-ax-lub .lub-thread} #### The Least Upper Bound Axiom Every non-empty subset of $\R$ that is bounded above has a least upper bound in $\R.$ ::: :::{.callout-tip} Note that using @thm-ax-lub to conclude that a subset $S\subseteq\R$ has a supremum requires $S$ to satisfy two hypotheses: - $S$ is nonempty, and - $S$ is bounded above. ::: From now on, we'll make a point of emphasizing which theorems that require the Least Upper Bound Axiom in their proofs and how. Theorems that rely on the Least Upper Bound Axiom will be highlighted with a [maroon]{.lub-hl} bar. As a first step to understanding least upper bounds, we should justify the use of the word "the" in @def-lub. :::{.callout-tip} Generally, when define a new quantity implicitly (i.e. without giving an *ex*plicit formula), we should ask ourselves whether a *unique* quantity satisfies the definition. For example, a subset of $\R$ may have more than one upper bound. That a subset has only one *least* upper bound (if it has one at all) is something we need to prove. ::: :::{.theorem #thm-lub-unique} If a subset of $\R$ has a least upper bound, then it is unique. ::: :::{.proof} Suppose $u,v$ are both least upper bounds for a subset $S\subseteq\R.$^[I am using one of the standard proof skeleta for proving uniqueness. See @sec-proof-skeleta.] Then $u$ is an upper bound for $S$, so since $v$ is a *least* upper bound, $v\leq u$. Also, $v$ is an upper bound for $S$, so since $u$ is the *least* upper bound, $u\leq v$. Therefore $u=v.$ ::: To prove a quantity is the least upper bound of a set, generally, one needs to show two things: prove that it's an upper bound, and then prove that any other upper bound is less than or equal to it. :::{.example #exm-sup-basic} Here we'll do a careful proof that $3$ is the supremum of $S=(-\infty,3)$. First, note that by definition $S$ is all numbers less than 3, so for any $x\in S$, $3>x.$ Therefore $3$ is an upper bound on $S$. By way of contradiction, suppose $b<3$ and $b$ is also an upper bound on $S$.^[In @exr-negate-lub you were to show that something like this is the negation of $3$ being the supremum. In logical symbols, saying 3 is the least upper bound is $(\forall \text{ upper bounds } b)(3\leq b)$. And thus its negation is $(\exists\text{ an upper bound } b)(b<3)$.] But then consider $x=\frac{b+3}{2}$ so that $bx$. ::: :::{.proof} By way of contradiction, suppose \_\_\_, which is the same as saying that $x$ is an upper bound on $\N$. Then since $\N$ is \_\_\_ and bounded above, $\N$ has a least upper bound $u.$ But then $u-\frac{1}{2}u-\frac{1}{2}+1=u+\frac{1}{2}>u,$$ and since $n+1\in\N$, we have contradicted \_\_\_. ::: :::{.exercise #exr-arch-fill-in-blanks} Fill in the blanks for the proof above. For the first blank, you'll need to negate the original statement. ::: :::{.proof .content-hidden} By way of contradiction, suppose there exists $x\in\R$ such that for all $n\in\N$, $n\leq x$. That is, suppose $x$ is an upper bound on $\N$. Then since $\N$ is nonempty and bounded above, $\N$ has a least upper bound $u=\sup(\N).$ But then $u-\frac{1}{2}u-\frac{1}{2}$. But then $m+1>u+\frac{1}{2}>u$, and since $m+1\in\N$, we have contradicted $u$ being the least upper bound. ::: :::{.corollary #cor-arch-prop} #### Archimedean Property - Version 2 For any $\epsilon>0$, there exists $n\in\N$ such that $\frac{1}{n}<\epsilon$. ::: :::{.exercise #exr-prove-arch-prop} Prove @cor-arch-prop. ::: :::{.callout-tip collapse="true"} #### Hint Remember that when we call a theorem a "Corollary", it means that you should use the preceeding theorem to prove it, and the proof will generally end up being quite short. ::: :::{.exercise #exr-careful-sup-proof} Give a careful proof that $1$ is the supremum of $S=\left\{\frac{n}{n+1}\st n\in\N\right\}$. Give a proof that is not a proof by contradiction. ::: :::{.callout-tip collapse="true"} #### Hint The same trick of taking an average like in @exm-sup-basic won't necessarily work since that average might not be in $S.$ @cor-arch-prop might be helpful here instead. ::: :::{.exercise #exr-sup-of-scalar-multiple} Suppose $S\subseteq\R$ is a nonempty set that is bounded above. Let $c>0$. We define the set $$T=\{cx\st x\in S\}.$$ Prove that $c\cdot\sup S$ is a least upper bound for $T$. ::: :::{.exercise #exr-sup-union} Suppose $S$ and $T$ are nonempty subsets of $\R$ that are bounded above. What is $\sup(S\cup T)?$ Prove your answer. ::: :::{.exercise #exr-arch-prop-no-lub} Prove the Archimedean Property is true when the universe is $\Q$. This means you'll need to refrain from making any references to $\R$, and you of course can't use the Least Upper Bound Axiom. ::: In Part 2e of @exr-lub-examples and the follow-up discussion, we claimed that the existence of suprema would "plug holes" in the real numbers. In particular, the supremum of $S=\{x\st x^2\leq 3\}$, which we are assuming exists because of the Least Upper Bound Axiom, seems like it ought to be a number whose square is 3. We'll prove a more general version of this fact below. :::{.theorem #thm-sqrts-exist .lub-thread} For any $a\geq 0$, there exists a real number $x\geq 0$ such that $x^2=a$. ::: :::{.proof} #### Proof Outline Suppose $a\geq 0$. Define $$S=\{y\st y^2\leq a\}$$ We will show that $S$ is nonempty and bounded above so that we can claim $\sup S$ exists. First, we note that $0^2=0a$$ So if $y\in S$, $$y^2\leq a< (a+1)^2$$ so $y
